original_abstract: In this paper, we present the Deep Gradient Network (DGNet), a novel deep learning framework designed to leverage object gradient supervision for camouflaged object detection (COD). The architecture uniquely decouples the detection process into two interconnected branches: a context encoder and a texture encoder. At the heart of this framework is a gradient-induced transition mechanism, which facilitates a "soft grouping" between contextual and textural features to better isolate hidden targets. Despite its architectural simplicity, DGNet significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art COD models across multiple benchmarks. Specifically, our streamlined version, DGNet-S, achieves real-time inference speeds of 80 fps while delivering performance comparable to the industry-leading JCSOD-CVPR21 model, despite utilizing only 6.82% of its parameters. Furthermore, we demonstrate the versatility of DGNet by achieving robust results in downstream applications, including polyp segmentation, industrial defect detection, and transparent object segmentation. Our code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/GewelsJI/DGNet.
humanized_abstract: Camouflaged Object Detection via Deep Gradient Network Camouflaged object detection (COD) is a newly emerging task that focuses on detecting objects rendered in a camouflage style, where objects and their surrounding environment exhibit a strong visual consistency. Most existing studies on COD rely on the annotation of object locations and the corresponding masks. However, such strong supervisory signals are often time-consuming and labor-intensive to obtain. In this work, we propose a Deep Gradient Network (DGNet) for camouflaged object detection by utilizing the object gradient supervision. The proposed DGNet is a simple yet effective deep learning framework. It consists of a context encoder and a texture encoder, which are connected by a gradient-induced transition mechanism for a “soft grouping” of contextual and textural features, facilitating the detection of camouflaged objects. Experimental results show that DGNet always achieves the best performance on three widely used COD benchmarks. Even when removing the semantic feature refinement component in DGNet, the remaining streamlined version, namely DGNet-S, can still maintain a real-time speed of 80 fps and surpasses the state-of-the-art JCSOD-CVPR21 model by a large margin with only 6.82% parameters. In addition, the proposed DGNet also shows robust performance in downstream tasks such as polyp segmentation, industrial defect detection, and transparent object segmentation. Our code and pre-trained models are available at https://github.com/GewelsJI/DGNet.
